plainMeaning: Reverence to the awakened ones who are the highest of all people — lions, pure white lotuses, and mighty fragrant elephants among human beings.

wordByWord.gloss: to the highest of men (purisa-uttama, 'best among persons')
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit purisa (Skt. puruṣa, 'man, person') + uttama ('highest, supreme'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ governed by the namo of the opening clause.
wordByWord.notes: First of the four 'purisa' epithets; the dative-of-homage continues from namotthuṇaṁ ('reverence be to…').
Tatpuruṣa simile: a lion among men — fearless, sovereign over the inner passion-beasts.

wordByWord.etymology: purisa ('man') + vara ('choicest, excellent'); first member of the following compound, here printed as a separate word.
wordByWord.notes: Intensifier 'vara' is attached only to the last two epithets, raising lotus and elephant to their 'choicest' kind.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit puṇḍarīa (Skt. puṇḍarīka, 'white lotus'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ; binds with the preceding purisavara.
wordByWord.notes: puṇḍarīka is specifically the white lotus that grows in mud yet is wholly unstained — the emblem of non-attachment.

wordByWord.gloss: best/most-excellent of men (repeated, opening the fourth epithet)
wordByWord.etymology: purisa + vara, as above; anaphoric repetition begins the gandhahasti epithet.
wordByWord.notes: The deliberate repetition of purisavara sustains the verse's anaphora.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt./Prakrit gandha ('smell, fragrance'); first member of gandha-hatthī.
wordByWord.notes: Joins with hatthī to form gandha-hasti, the rutting 'scent-elephant'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit hatthī (Skt. hastin, 'elephant'); genitive plural -āṇaṁ → -īṇaṁ; completes purisavara-gandhahatthīṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: gandha-hastin = the musth tusker whose scent alone scatters rival elephants — the verse's final and densest image.

literalTranslation: (Reverence) to the highest of men, to the lions among men, to the best-of-men white lotuses, to the best-of-men fragrant elephants.
idiomaticTranslation: Reverence to those who are supreme among all human beings — lions in courage, white lotuses in unstained purity, and the noblest of fragrant elephants in majesty — among men.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the previous clause has placed the Arihantas by what they did — founding the ford, awakening unaided — this clause places them by what they ARE among living beings, before the next clause widens the lens to the whole cosmos (the loga- titles). The single stem purisa- ('man') is repeated four times so the listener feels the Arihanta's pre-eminence first within humanity, the very order of beings into which a Tīrthaṅkara is born. The opening title is plain superlative — purisuttama, simply 'highest of men'. The three that follow are similes, each more concrete: the lion (sīha), the white lotus (puṇḍarīka), and the fragrant elephant (gandha-hastī), the last two heightened by vara, 'choicest of its kind'. The lion names fearlessness and unchallenged sovereignty — the Arihanta who has subdued the beasts of passion. The white lotus, rising spotless from mud and water, names the supreme paradox of the Jina: born and living in the world, yet wholly untouched by it — perfect non-attachment. The gandha-hastī, the musth tusker whose very scent makes ordinary elephants turn and flee, names an irresistible, calming majesty: tradition holds that calamity, enmity, and disease retreat in the Jina's presence as lesser elephants retreat before the scent-elephant. The progression is deliberate — superlative, then courage, then purity, then commanding benevolent power — a complete portrait of supremacy among men, drawn entirely in the idiom of the noblest creatures of the Indian imagination.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The four titles are not flattery but a doctrinal claim about the Arihanta's standing among the four destinies (gati): liberation is won from the human state, so supremacy 'among men' marks the summit of saṁsāric existence from which omniscience is reached. The white-lotus image encodes the cardinal Jain principle of bondage-by-attachment: it is not contact with the world but rāga-dveṣa (attraction-aversion) that stains, and the Jina, like the puṇḍarīka, lives untouched. The gandha-hastī image expresses the atiśaya (supernormal influence) of a Tīrthaṅkara — the harmonizing power radiating from a soul that has destroyed the ghātī karmas.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: The reciter is given four meditative images to hold rather than abstractions: when bowing, picture courage without aggression (lion), purity that engagement cannot soil (lotus), and a presence that quiets conflict (fragrant elephant) — and take these as the measure of one's own aspiration in daily conduct.

crossReferences.text: puṇḍarīka 'white lotus' and gandha-hastin 'scent-elephant' as Jina-similes
crossReferences.source: Monier-Williams, A Sanskrit-English Dictionary (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)

sources.consultedFor: Root glosses for puruṣa, uttama, siṁha, puṇḍarīka (white lotus), and gandha-hastin (rutting scent-elephant).

sources.title: Namutthunam / Śakra-stava text and commentary repository
sources.consultedFor: Traditional reading of the purisa-cluster and the gandhahastī symbolism (rival elephants and afflictions retreating in the Jina's presence).

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi Dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth — digital library
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms purisa, sīha, puṇḍarīa, hatthī and the genitive-plural -āṇaṁ inflection.

sources.title: Ṇamōkāra / Namaskāra tradition and Ardhamāgadhī orthography
sources.consultedFor: Ardhamāgadhī canonical context and the -ṇaṁ/-nam homage formula in early Jain praise-litanies.

notes: Verse is verbatim from the book, including word-internal spacing ('પુરિસવર પુંડરીઆણં', 'પુરિસવર ગંધ હત્થીણં') which other editions print solid as purisavarapuṇḍarīāṇaṁ / purisavaragandhahatthīṇaṁ; the spacing does not change the compound sense. All four epithets are datives/genitives of homage continuing the namotthuṇaṁ of clause 1 (verse 2). The gandha-hastī's 'afflictions/rivals flee within a yojana' reading is the standard commentarial gloss reported across sources but is interpretive imagery rather than a literal clause of this verse; flagged as such. 100% token coverage; the two occurrences of પુરિસવર are glossed separately as printed.
